questions.question: Explain the var keyword introduced in Java 10. What is its primary purpose?
keyPoints: var is a reserved type name introduced in Java 10
It enables local variable type inference
Primary purpose is to reduce boilerplate code
Maintains compile-time type safety (not like dynamic typing)
Eliminates need to write explicit types for local variables

questions.question: List three restrictions on using the var keyword in Java.
keyPoints: Cannot be used for method parameters
Cannot be used for instance variables
Cannot be used for return types
Must be initialized at declaration (cannot be left uninitialized)
Cannot be used with array initializers directly
Cannot initialize with null value

questions.question: What happens when var is used with the diamond operator (<>) in Java? Explain with an example.
keyPoints: When var is used with diamond operator, compiler defaults to Object type
Example: var list = new ArrayList<>(); results in ArrayList<Object>
Generic type information is lost when not specified
Recommendation: Always specify type parameters when using var

questions.question: Write a Java code snippet demonstrating var usage in: (a) for loops (b) for-each loops (c) try-with-resources
keyPoints: (a) Traditional: for(var i = 0; i < 10; i++)
(b) Enhanced: for(var item : collection)
(c) try(var reader = new FileReader("file.txt"))
All three contexts are valid in Java 10+

questions.question: Differentiate between var and Object type declaration in Java with an example.
keyPoints: var preserves exact compile-time type
Object loses generic type information
Example: var list = new ArrayList<String>() → ArrayList<String>
Object list = new ArrayList<String>() → Object (type erased)
var maintains type safety, Object does not

questions.question: Explain the type inference mechanism of the var keyword in Java. How does it differ from dynamically typed languages? Include code examples to illustrate.
keyPoints: var uses compile-time type inference from right-hand side expression
Compiler analyzes the initializer expression to determine exact type
Type is fixed at compile time - no runtime overhead
Differs from dynamic typing: Java remains statically typed
Dynamic languages determine type at runtime, var determines at compile time
Code example showing var preserves types vs dynamic typing

questions.question: Write a Java program that demonstrates various valid and invalid uses of the var keyword. Include examples of correct initialization, usage in loops, lambda expressions, and common mistakes that cause compile-time errors.
keyPoints: Valid: var x = 10; var str = "Hello"; var list = new ArrayList<String>();
Valid in loops: for(var i = 0; i < n; i++), for(var item : items)
Lambda with var: (var a, var b) -> a + b (Java 11+)
Invalid: var uninitialized; var arr = {1,2,3}; var for parameter
Compile errors shown for each invalid case
Explanation of why each is invalid

questions.question: Discuss the advantages and limitations of using the var keyword in Java. When should a developer prefer using explicit type declarations over var?
keyPoints: Advantages: Reduces verbosity, improves readability with complex generics, maintains type safety, backward compatible
Limitations: Cannot use with fields/parameters, loses clarity for primitives, diamond operator defaults to Object, reduced readability for complex inferred types
Prefer explicit types when: Generic types are complex, public API exposed, documentation clarity needed, beginners reading code
